my_thoughts: The constraint_collapse mechanism works: tests ultimate limit (Achilles = maximum external goods), exposes irrelevance (weeping for a girl = trivial passion defeats maximum). The student's final status appeal (lineage vanity) shows they're still grasping at external validation. This is the final cut—even the maximum exemplar is shown to be defeated by triviality. The pattern is complete: escalation through all external goods → comparison to mythic exemplars → collapse showing irrelevance. Hypothesis: constraint collapse at maximum exemplar level produces recognition of external goods' irrelevance—testable by tracking whether students recognize the futility after this final comparison.
accumulated_insight: Five-turn pattern: T1: Student says 'But I also am rich'—seeking validation via bragging, external validation. The comparison_contrast mechanism uses mythological benchmark (Agamemnon), tests limit. T2: Student says 'But I am also handsome'—transitions to persistent bragging. Comparison contrast repeats benchmark (Achilles). T3: Student says 'But I have also beautiful hair'—transitions to unknown state. Comparison contrast continues. T4: Student says 'But I am also strong'—transitions to desperate validation. Comparison contrast continues. T5: Student says 'But I am also of noble birth'—final status appeal via lineage vanity. The constraint_collapse mechanism tests ultimate limit: even Achilles (maximum external goods) is defeated by trivial passion (weeping for a girl). Pattern complete: bragging escalation → comparison to mythic exemplars → final collapse showing external goods are irrelevant. They've shifted: external status = irrelevant (constraint collapse).
visible_response: Are you the son of a goddess mother? are you the son of a father sprung from Zeus? What good then do these things do to him, when he sits and weeps for a girl?
